meta.title: 6.5 Cost Observability and Showback
meta.description: Attribute Make's infrastructure costs across compute, model inference, storage, and network using resource tagging and per-session AI cost logging — so you can answer 'where is the $2M going' with feature-level precision and make model tier tradeoffs with unit economics.

blocks.prompt:
Make's AI infrastructure costs $2M/month. Leadership asks: "Where is the money going?" Can you answer with precision, or is it a black box?

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

By the end of this module, you'll be able to design a three-layer cost observability system — attribution tagging, unit economics dashboards, and showback reporting — and use it to identify model routing opportunities, evaluate pricing tier sustainability, and diagnose per-session cost trends before they become a structural problem.

Without resource tagging and per-session AI cost logging, the answer to 'where is the $2M going' is a cloud vendor invoice — cost observability requires attributing every infrastructure action to a feature and user segment before any optimization or pricing decision can be grounded in unit economics. AI infrastructure costs are high, variable, and opaque by default. Without deliberate cost attribution, the answer to "where is the money going" is a shrug and a cloud vendor invoice.

This module builds the mental model for cost observability — not accounting, but understanding unit economics well enough to make roadmap tradeoffs and model pricing strategy.

---

**The Four Cost Dimensions**

AI platforms have four distinct cost categories that behave very differently:

**Compute:** The servers running preview environments, build pipelines, and dev servers. Costs scale with active sessions. Predictable during business hours, volatile during traffic spikes. Managed through autoscaling and instance right-sizing.

**AI (model inference):** Token-based pricing from Anthropic (or whichever model provider). Costs scale with the number and length of agent sessions, and the model tier used. This is often the largest and fastest-growing cost category. Highly sensitive to prompt design decisions.

**Storage:** User project files, cached npm dependencies, build artifacts, preview snapshots. Costs scale with user count and project count. Lower growth rate than compute or AI but compounds over time.

**Network:** CDN bandwidth for serving previews, egress costs for API calls to external services, data transfer between regions. Often overlooked until it's significant.

Understanding the breakdown matters because each dimension has a different optimization strategy and a different owner on the engineering team.

---

**Per-Feature Cost Attribution**

At the cloud vendor level, you get one bill. Cost observability requires tagging infrastructure resources so costs can be attributed to features and user actions.

Every preview environment, every build job, every agent session should carry metadata tags:

```
feature: "preview"
user_tier: "pro"
region: "us-east-1"
template_type: "react-vite"
```

With consistent tagging, you can answer: How much does the "preview" feature cost per day? How does preview cost vary by template type — does a Next.js preview cost twice as much as a vanilla React preview because of the build pipeline complexity?

For AI costs specifically, every model API call should log:
- Input tokens
- Output tokens
- Model tier (Haiku, Sonnet, Opus)
- Feature context (agent session, error translation, build summary)
- Session ID (for per-session cost rollup)

This data, aggregated, tells you the average cost per agent session — and lets you see when that number trends up (a sign of prompt bloat or context window mismanagement).

blocks.question: You've added cost attribution tags to all preview environments and agent sessions. Two weeks later, you notice that React + Tailwind previews cost 1.8x more per session than vanilla HTML previews. What are the two most likely explanations, and what data would you pull to distinguish between them?
blocks.answer: The two most likely explanations are: (1) compute — the React/Tailwind build pipeline takes longer and consumes more CPU/memory (more npm dependencies to install, webpack compilation step), and (2) AI — the agent generates more code turns for React projects, consuming more tokens. To distinguish: pull the build duration and CPU-minute metrics for both template types (compute hypothesis) and pull the average input/output tokens per session by template type (AI hypothesis). If build duration is 2x longer for React templates, compute is the driver. If token counts per session are higher, the agent is taking more turns — likely because the React scaffold is more complex and generates more back-and-forth.

blocks.content:
---

**Per-User Cost Profiles**

Not all users cost the same. A power user running 20 agent sessions per day, each building a complex multi-page app, might cost 50x more than a casual user who opens Make twice a week to update a landing page.

Cost profile segmentation reveals:
- Whether your pricing tiers align with your cost structure (are Pro plan users actually more profitable than Free users, or are they your most expensive customers?)
- Which user behaviors drive disproportionate cost (long agent sessions, high dependency counts, frequent preview refreshes)
- Where you need usage limits or metering to protect unit economics

This is the input to pricing strategy. If the average Pro user costs $8/month to serve and pays $20/month, your contribution margin is positive. If the top 10% of Pro users cost $60/month to serve and you're charging them $20/month, you have a structural problem that grows with scale.

---

**AI Cost Optimization**

AI inference costs are the most controllable cost dimension with the highest leverage. Key optimization levers:

**Model routing:** Not every task requires your most capable model. A request to "change the button color to blue" doesn't need the same model as "architect a multi-step checkout flow with state management." Route simple, well-defined tasks to faster, cheaper models (Claude Haiku). Route complex, generative tasks to more capable models (Claude Sonnet). A well-designed routing layer can reduce inference costs 40-60% with minimal quality impact on simple tasks.

**Prompt caching:** Anthropic's API supports caching of system prompts. If Make's system prompt is 4,000 tokens and you're running 50,000 sessions per day, caching eliminates the input token cost for that prompt prefix across all sessions after the first. At $3/million tokens, that's meaningful savings at scale.

**Context pruning:** Agent sessions accumulate conversation history. By session turn 10, you're sending 8,000 tokens of history to get a response about a single button color. Intelligent context pruning — summarizing older turns rather than sending them verbatim — reduces input token costs without significant quality loss.

**Batch inference:** For non-real-time tasks (nightly eval runs, session quality scoring), batch inference APIs offer 50% cost reduction with higher latency. Run your eval suite on batch inference overnight instead of real-time.

---

**Showback Dashboards**

"Showback" is the practice of making internal cost attribution visible to the teams that drive it, without requiring them to pay for it (that would be "chargeback," a different and more politically fraught model).

For Make, showback means:
- The agent infrastructure team can see what their feature costs per session and per month
- The preview team can see the compute and CDN cost breakdown for their feature
- Product managers can model the cost impact of proposed features before they're built

A showback dashboard should answer: "If we add server-side rendering support for previews, how much will that increase compute costs per session, and what does that do to our unit economics at 100K DAU?"

This makes cost a first-class input to product decisions, not a surprise on the quarterly cloud bill.

---

**Unit Economics**

The three most important unit economics metrics for a Platform PM to own:

- **Cost per preview:** total preview infrastructure cost ÷ total preview loads. Target: under $0.005.
- **Cost per agent session:** total AI + compute cost per session ÷ total sessions. Target: monitor and trend.
- **Cost per deployment:** total deployment infrastructure cost ÷ total deployments. Target: under $0.10.

These numbers, tracked over time, tell you whether your platform is getting more or less efficient as it scales. A cost per session that drops as volume grows indicates healthy economies of scale. One that rises suggests architectural issues or prompt bloat.

---

## How to Think About This

Total monthly cost is a symptom; unit economics are the diagnosis. A $2M monthly bill tells you nothing about efficiency. Cost per agent session, tracked as a time series, tells you whether the platform is getting more or less efficient as it scales — a rising cost per session is a signal that something inside sessions is growing faster than volume (context window bloat, model tier drift, session length creep). Showback works on the same principle: engineers who can see that a new prompt section costs $40K per month in aggregate reason differently about prompt design than engineers who see only an undifferentiated infrastructure bill.

blocks.question: Your AI cost per agent session has increased 35% over the past 6 weeks. There has been no change in model pricing. What are your top three hypotheses, and what data would you pull to test each?
blocks.answer:
*Look for: (1) Context window growth — are sessions using more tokens per turn? Check average input/output tokens per session over time. (2) Model tier drift — are more requests being routed to expensive models? Check model tier distribution. (3) Session length increase — are users taking more turns per session? Check turns per session distribution.*

---

blocks.title:
Find the pricing page for Anthropic's Claude API. Calculate: if Make runs 100,000 agent sessions per day, each consuming an average of 8,000 input tokens and 2,000 output tokens using Claude Sonnet, what is the monthly model inference cost? Now calculate what 40% model routing to Haiku for simple tasks would save. This is the kind of back-of-envelope math that justifies model routing investment.

---
blocks.solution: Using current Anthropic pricing (check the page for exact figures, as rates change): Claude Sonnet input tokens cost approximately $3/million and output approximately $15/million. For 100,000 sessions/day × 30 days = 3,000,000 sessions/month. At 8,000 input tokens per session: 3,000,000 × 8,000 = 24 billion input tokens = $72,000/month in input costs. At 2,000 output tokens: 3,000,000 × 2,000 = 6 billion output tokens = $90,000/month. Total: approximately $162,000/month. With 40% routing to Haiku for simple tasks: Haiku input costs roughly $0.25/million and output $1.25/million. Routing 40% of sessions to Haiku (1,200,000 sessions/month) saves approximately (($3 - $0.25) × 9.6B input tokens / 1M) + (($15 - $1.25) × 2.4B output tokens / 1M) ≈ $26,400 + $33,000 = ~$59,000/month — roughly 36% of total inference cost. This back-of-envelope calculation is the business case for building a model routing layer. Check current pricing before presenting to leadership, as token costs shift.

blocks.prompt:
Explain "showback dashboards" to a VP of Engineering who has never seen one. Why does making cost visible to feature teams (without making them pay for it) change their behavior?

---

questions: Think of a product or engineering decision you've been involved in where cost visibility — or the lack of it — shaped the outcome. Was there a moment where someone would have made a different decision if they'd known what the infrastructure cost was? What was the equivalent of the 'cloud vendor invoice problem'?

On an AI or ML product you've worked on or studied, was there per-feature or per-user cost attribution? If not — if it was all one undifferentiated infrastructure bill — what questions couldn't you answer as a result? What would you have built first to close that gap?

Recall a situation where you had to make a pricing or packaging decision for a product. How well did you understand the cost structure behind the features you were pricing? Looking back, what cost observability would have made that decision more grounded?

---

blocks.content:
Cost observability requires tagging every infrastructure action with feature and user metadata, then building showback dashboards that make unit economics visible to the teams that drive them — because you can only optimize what you can see.

---

blocks.prompt:
You've built the monitoring to know things are degrading. But what do you do when they actually break? Incident response for AI systems has unique failure modes that traditional runbooks miss.

---

reviewQuestions.question: What are the four distinct cost dimensions the module identifies for AI platforms like Make?
options: Engineering headcount, cloud infrastructure, third-party APIs, and security tooling
Compute, AI model inference, storage, and network
Frontend hosting, backend compute, database storage, and CDN bandwidth
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module identifies four cost categories that behave differently: Compute (servers running preview environments and build pipelines, scales with active sessions), AI/model inference (token-based pricing, often the largest and fastest-growing), Storage (user projects, cached npm packages, build artifacts), and Network (CDN bandwidth, API egress, cross-region transfer).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Good recall! Compute, AI inference, storage, and network — each has different growth patterns and different optimization strategies.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The module's four cost dimensions are: Compute (preview environments and build servers), AI inference (token costs from the model provider), Storage (user files and build artifacts), and Network (CDN and API egress). Each behaves differently and has a different optimization owner.

reviewQuestions.question: Which AI cost optimization technique does the module estimate can reduce inference costs by 40–60% with minimal quality impact?
options: Context pruning — summarizing older conversation turns instead of sending them verbatim
Batch inference — running all agent sessions overnight at 50% lower cost
Model routing — directing simple tasks to cheaper models (Haiku) and complex tasks to more capable models (Sonnet)
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module states that model routing — using the right model tier for the task — can reduce inference costs 40–60% with minimal quality impact on simple tasks. A request to change a button color doesn't need the same model as architecting a multi-step checkout flow. Batch inference offers 50% cost reduction but only applies to non-real-time tasks.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it — model routing is the highest-leverage inference cost optimization, with a 40–60% reduction estimate.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Context pruning and batch inference are both valid techniques, but the module attributes the 40–60% cost reduction estimate specifically to model routing — matching the model tier to the complexity of the task rather than routing everything through the most capable (and most expensive) model.

reviewQuestions.question: What is the module's target for cost per preview — the unit economics target a Platform PM should track?
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module lists the three most important unit economics metrics and their targets: cost per preview (under $0.005), cost per agent session (monitor and trend), and cost per deployment (under $0.10). These numbers, tracked over time, reveal whether the platform is becoming more or less efficient at scale.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice — $0.005 per preview is the target. Unit economics at this level make the platform viable at scale.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The module specifies under $0.005 (half a cent) per preview load — not $0.05 or $0.50. At 50,000 daily preview loads, even a small per-unit cost compounds significantly, which is why the target is set at fractions of a cent.
